retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccal Nerve -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Another possible option is an inferior alveolar nerve block, using the so-called anterior technique, which involves anesthetizing the buccal nerve. This technique uses the injection of the anesthetic into the pterygomaxillary space in the area anterior to the mandibular foramen and only infiltrating one ampoule of anesthetic. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Alveolar Nerve -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dentists commonly inject the superior and inferior alveolar nerves with local anesthetic for dental procedures. The knowledge of the location of these nerves will guide a successful nerve block. But it is crucial to take into consideration the many variations in the nerves. If the classic approach to the nerve block is insufficient, the operator should consider that maybe there is a slight variation in the location of the alveolar nerves. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve and Lingual Nerve Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetic injections are performed prior to most surgical procedures, and both the IAN and LN may be injured during attempts to place blocks around them (see Figure. Conventional Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block). It is very rare, but the needle or the local anesthetic can cause direct or indirect damage to the nerve. The risk is especially increased during the IAN block because the needle travels in close proximity to both the IAN and the LN as they descend medial to the ramus of the mandible. Three theories may explain how nerve damage occurs in these situations. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Medial (Internal) Pterygoid Nerve -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Effective local anesthesia is achievable by injecting the anesthetic solution into the tissue surrounding the nerve innervating the area treated. In some cases, adequate anesthesia is not attainable. This failure may be due to anatomical anomalies/variations. Thus, thorough knowledge of normal and probable physiologic variants is necessary. Various studies have demonstrated the anatomic variation of the mandibular nerve, but thus far studies have not reported differences, particularly of the nerve to medial pterygoid.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Location of Mandibular Foramen
retrieved_docs.content: For an effective IANB, the local anesthetic solution must be deposited near the nerve before it enters the mandibular foramen. Several studies have indicated that depositing the local anesthetic molecules into the pterygomandibular space can achieve the desired anesthesia. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical importance of the mandibular foramen comes into play for nerve blocks. The inferior alveolar nerve is commonly a target for blocking during dental procedures. [14] [15] The location the inferior alveolar nerve block is usually before it enters the mandibular foramen. The landmarks for the inferior alveolar nerve block is the pterygomandibular raphe and the coronoid notch. [7] The local anesthetic is usually injected between these two structures to successful numb the sensory territory of the inferior alveolar nerve. In nerve blocking of the inferior alveolar nerve, the clinician should account for the age of the patient due to the location variation of the nerve in adults and children. [4] Also, it is important to account for the possible variations, such as when there are multiple inferior alveolar nerves. Using radiographic imaging, the clinician can confirm the presences of multiple mandibular foramina, since the number of mandibular foramina corresponds to the number of inferior alveolar nerves. This approach ensures a proper and successful nerve block that would anesthetize the entire region innervated by the inferior alveolar nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasopalatine Nerve -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The technique traditionally involved a single penetration into the canal, which was too traumatic and painful for the patient. The current practice entails multiple injections, with the first one performed on the labial side in the interdental papilla, entering horizontally with a 30-gauge needle. The solution diffuses to the palate after achieving buccal anesthesia. However, this block cannot be accomplished in patients with complete cleft palates, as they have malformed premaxillae. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Anatomy of the face is essential when it comes to surgeries in the face. The face is highly vascularized and heavily innervated by nerves. The knowledge of the anatomy of the face can guide clinicians in the avoidance of damage to crucial structures in the face during surgeries and injections in the face.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Medial Pterygoid Muscle -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: During nerve block procedure for the mandibular arch, the area where the needle should focus is occupied to a large extent by the medial pterygoid muscle. During an inferior alveolar nerve block, if the needle placement is too medial, there are chances that the needle might pierce the medial pterygoid muscle resulting in less effective anesthesia as well as postoperative trismus. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Facial Nerve
retrieved_docs.content: The temporal branch travels superiorly to innervate the frontalis and orbicularis oculi's superior aspect. The zygomatic and buccal branches course deep below the masseteric fascia. These nerves pass near the dissection plane and are thus at a higher risk of injury during subperiosteal dissection for zygomatic implant placement. The zygomatic branch innervates the orbicularis oculi's inferior aspect and nasal area muscles. The buccal branch innervates the upper lip and cheek muscles. The marginal mandibular nerve exits the parotid at the mandibular angle and follows the angle's inferior border. Injury to this nerve weakens the mentalis and depressor anguli oris—the muscles that move the lower lip. The facial nerve's cervical branch innervates the platysma. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Nerve Anatomy and Clinical Applications -- Surgical Considerations -- Parotid Gland Surgery and Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: During parotid gland surgery, branches of the facial nerve may be damaged as the nerve branches off within the parotid gland. After parotid surgery, around 50% develop temporary facial weakness, while 7% end up with permanent facial palsy. [11] Also, an inferior alveolar nerve block during dental procedures may cause facial palsy if the injection goes too deep into the parotid gland. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve and Lingual Nerve Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The injection needle may cause direct axonal trauma if it comes in contact with or penetrates the nerve. Another theory is that the needle may damage the epineural blood vessels and cause a localized hematoma, causing a compression injury to the nerve or forming reactive fibrosis or scar. The last hypothesis is that the local anesthetic itself can cause cytotoxic injuries as the chemicals are metabolized.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment -- Conventional Technique
retrieved_docs.content: The syringe barrel is positioned over the premolars on the side opposite the injection. The insertion point lies along an imaginary line extending from the deepest part of the pterygomandibular raphe to the coronoid notch. The exact location of the entry point is one-fourth the distance toward the raphe above the occlusal level of mandibular teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Nerve -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The following branches of the mandibular nerve efferently serve the muscles of mastication which include the medial and lateral pterygoids, masseter, and the temporalis. The nerve to the medial pterygoid provides innervation to its respective muscle which is responsible for the elevation of the mandible (closes the jaw). The nerve to temporalis provides innervation to the temporalis muscle which is responsible for elevation and retrusion of the mandible (closes and retracts the jaw). The masseteric nerve supplies the masseter muscle which elevates and protrudes the jaw. The nerve to the lateral pterygoid provides innervation to the lateral pterygoid muscle which depresses the mandible. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.content: The surface and applied anatomy of the forehead and the extent of the frontalis muscle must be appreciated when performing surgery in this region or injecting toxins. ( Fig 5)

It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1232
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of neurolytic agent into sympathetic nerve is regarded as a type of Other non‑OR or closed therapeutic nervous system procedure. The category Other non‑OR or closed therapeutic nervous system procedures is itself classified as blokáda sympatických nervů (sympathetic nerve block) and functions to classify other non‑OR or closed therapeutic nervous system procedures within the same therapeutic domain. In addition, the procedural actions incision and excision of CNS are linked to the skull (lebka), and the skull (lebka) is associated with Other non‑OR or closed therapeutic nervous system procedures, indicating that such closed therapeutic interventions may involve cranial structures or be relevant to procedures performed in or around the skull. This network of classifications and relationships highlights how neurolytic sympathetic nerve injections, broader closed therapeutic nervous system procedures, and cranial surgical actions are interconnected within the framework of nervous system interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 547
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of locally‑acting therapeutic substance into the trachea is classified as a form of throat surgery. Throat surgery, in turn, is encompassed within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum. This hierarchical relationship indicates that delivering therapeutic agents directly into the trachea is both a surgical intervention on the throat and part of the spectrum of diagnostic techniques used to evaluate and manage conditions of the respiratory system and mediastinal structures, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate procedural approaches for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1065
retrieved_docs.content: Incision and excision of CNS is closely linked to the skull (lebka), as surgical access to the central nervous system typically requires opening or removing portions of the cranial bone. The skull (lebka) is also associated with a cranial nerve disorder (اضطراب بالعصب القحفي), reflecting the anatomical relationship between the bony structure and the nerves that traverse it. This cranial nerve disorder (اضطراب بالعصب القحفي) is categorized under the broader condition of ocular migraine (الشقيقة الشالة للعين), indicating that disturbances of cranial nerves can be manifestations of or related to ocular migraine. Recognizing how the procedure, skull anatomy, cranial nerve pathology, and ocular migraine interrelate aids clinicians in planning neurosurgical interventions, diagnosing nerve‑related symptoms, and providing comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 978
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of a neurolytic agent into the sympathetic nerve is categorized as an “Other non‑OR or closed therapeutic nervous system procedure.” This category also includes procedures described as “blokáda sympatických nervů” (sympathetic nerve block), indicating that sympathetic nerve blockade is considered a type of non‑operating‑room or closed therapeutic intervention on the nervous system. The “Other non‑OR or closed therapeutic nervous system procedures” category further classifies itself, reinforcing its role as a distinct group of therapeutic techniques that are performed outside the traditional operating‑room setting. In addition, surgical actions such as incision and excision of the central nervous system are linked to the skull (“lebka”), highlighting the anatomical focus of these procedures on cranial structures. The skull itself is associated with both “اضطراب بالعصب القحفي” (disorder of the cranial nerve) and the broader group of “Other non‑OR or closed therapeutic nervous system procedures,” suggesting that cranial nerve disorders may be addressed through such minimally invasive, non‑operating‑room interventions. The cranial nerve disorder is further related to “الشقيقة الشالة للعين” (ocular migraine), indicating that ocular migraine can be considered a parent condition or overarching clinical context for certain cranial nerve disturbances. Together, these relationships illustrate how neurolytic sympathetic nerve injections, sympathetic nerve blocks, and other closed therapeutic nervous system procedures are integrated within cranial anatomy and linked to specific neurological disorders such as cranial nerve dysfunction and ocular migraine, informing both diagnostic considerations and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. The specific intervention “Injection of larynx” includes as a subordinate step the procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway). Likewise, the broader category “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” also lists “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” as a child procedure. This demonstrates that placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway is a common subprocedure both within targeted laryngeal injections and within general non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, underscoring its relevance to airway management across multiple procedural contexts.
